title: AZ-400 — Azure DevOps Engineer Expert Practice Exam

questions.stem: Which DevOps practice most directly reduces it works on my machine issues?
questions.explanation: IaC means dev/stage/prod come from the same code. Standups and estimation are process tools but don't address environment drift directly.

questions.stem: DORA's four key metrics include all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: DORA: Deployment frequency, Lead time, Change failure rate, MTTR (plus Reliability in newer reports). Story points are Agile.

questions.stem: Which Azure Boards work-item type sits ABOVE a User Story in the default Agile process?
questions.explanation: Agile hierarchy: Epic → Feature → User Story → Task / Bug.

questions.stem: Which are typical components of an Azure DevOps organization? (Choose three.)
questions.explanation: Resource Groups are an Azure (cloud) concept, not Azure DevOps.

questions.stem: To link a Git commit to an Azure Boards work item #42, include in the commit message:
questions.explanation: AB#42 is the syntax Azure Boards parses from commit messages and PR titles.

questions.stem: The recommended cadence for Scrum sprints is typically:
questions.explanation: Scrum sprints are 1-4 weeks; 2 weeks is most common.

questions.stem: The Azure Boards process template (Basic, Agile, Scrum, CMMI) is selected at project creation and easily switched later.
questions.explanation: Selected at creation, but switching requires careful migration — work item types and fields differ.

questions.stem: Which branching strategy works best with continuous deployment, feature flags, and small batches?
questions.explanation: Trunk-based dev pairs naturally with CD: small frequent merges to main, features dark behind flags.

questions.stem: To prevent direct pushes to `main` in Azure Repos, configure:

options.text: A Git hook on every dev's machine
Branch policy with require pull request

questions.explanation: Branch policies enforce PR-driven changes server-side. Local hooks are bypassable.

questions.stem: Which can be required by an Azure Repos branch policy? (Choose three.)
questions.explanation: A, B, C are built-in policies. Commit message length is not enforced as a branch policy in Azure Repos.

questions.stem: CODEOWNERS-style automatic reviewers by path in Azure Repos is configured in:

options.text: A CODEOWNERS file in the repo
The branch policies of the target branch

questions.explanation: Azure Repos uses branch policies → Automatically include reviewers with path filters. GitHub uses CODEOWNERS file.

questions.stem: To avoid storing large binaries in Git history, use:
questions.explanation: Git LFS keeps binary blobs in a separate store and points to them from Git.

questions.stem: In Azure Repos, branch policies are configured on the:
questions.explanation: Policies live on the destination branch (the one being merged INTO).

questions.stem: Which merge strategy keeps `main` history linear with one commit per PR?
questions.explanation: Squash merge condenses the PR's commits into one. Rebase merge keeps each commit but linearizes them.

questions.stem: In Azure Pipelines, PR triggers must be defined in the YAML of the target branch, not the source branch.
questions.explanation: Move the `pr:` config to `main` first; PR validations don't fire from feature-branch YAML.

questions.stem: Which YAML expression evaluates at compile time (when YAML is parsed)?
questions.explanation: ${{ }} is compile-time. $() is runtime variable substitution. $[ ] is a runtime expression.

questions.stem: To use a saved plan file across stages, you should:

options.text: Save it as a pipeline variable
Publish it as a pipeline artifact and download: in the next stage
Email it to the next runner

questions.explanation: PublishPipelineArtifact@1 + download: current is the cross-stage handoff pattern.

questions.stem: Pipeline templates support all of the following template types EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Step, job, stage, and variable templates exist. There is no pool template type.

questions.stem: To restrict an org to a pipeline template (e.g., for SAST scanning compliance), use:
questions.explanation: Project settings → Pipelines → Required pipeline template enforces extends: from a specific template.

questions.stem: Microsoft-hosted agents are best when: (Choose two.)

options.text: You need access to a private VNet for build dependencies
You don't want to maintain VMs
You need clean, isolated builds with no leaked state

questions.explanation: Microsoft-hosted = no maintenance, fresh-per-job. Private VNet access and GPUs typically need self-hosted.

questions.stem: The recommended modern approach for autoscaling self-hosted agents in Azure is:

options.text: Azure DevOps Scale Set agents backed by Azure VMSS

questions.explanation: Scale Set agent pools automatically resize the underlying VMSS based on queued jobs.

questions.stem: Which Docker layer ordering maximizes cache hits in a Node.js Dockerfile?

options.text: COPY all source first, then RUN npm ci
COPY package*.json, RUN npm ci, then COPY remaining source
RUN npm ci before any COPY

questions.explanation: Copying only the package files first lets npm ci's layer cache survive source changes.

questions.stem: Best practice for tagging an image built in CI is:

options.text: Tag with the commit SHA, plus optionally a semantic-version tag
Don't tag — let the registry assign

questions.explanation: Immutable tags (commit SHA) give traceability. :latest is fine as a secondary tag for dev, never for prod deploy.

questions.stem: Which is the modern, recommended way for an Azure DevOps pipeline to authenticate to Azure?

options.text: Storing a Service Principal password as a secret variable
Service connection with Workload Identity Federation (OIDC)
Storing the user's AAD password in Key Vault

questions.explanation: OIDC = no long-lived secret stored in Azure DevOps. Microsoft's recommendation for all new service connections.

questions.stem: Which Azure Pipelines feature lets you require human approval before a stage runs?
questions.explanation: Define an environment in Pipelines → Environments → Approvals & checks. Deployment jobs target that environment.

questions.stem: To cache npm dependencies between pipeline runs, use:

options.text: Cache@2 task with a key based on package-lock.json

questions.explanation: Cache@2 with a key derived from the lockfile maximizes cache hit and invalidates on dep changes.

questions.stem: Which are valid stages-level constructs in Azure Pipelines YAML? (Choose three.)
questions.explanation: pool is set on jobs, not stages. dependsOn, condition, and jobs are stage-level.

questions.stem: Which container registry tier is required for geo-replication and private endpoints in ACR?
questions.explanation: Geo-replication, content trust, private endpoints, and repository-scoped tokens are Premium-only.

questions.stem: Which deployment strategy keeps a complete second environment idle until a switch?
questions.explanation: Blue/Green = two parallel environments. Switch traffic atomically; instant rollback.

questions.stem: App Service deployment slot swap is essentially:

options.text: A metadata change that flips routing — near-instant

questions.explanation: The compute doesn't move; only the slot's identity flips. Hence the instant rollback by swapping back.

questions.stem: Canary deployment refers to:

options.text: Sending a small percentage of traffic to a new version, ramping up over time

questions.explanation: Canary = progressive traffic shift with monitoring; auto-rollback if metrics regress.

questions.stem: Which are valid Kubernetes deployment strategies? (Choose two.)
questions.explanation: Built-in: Recreate and RollingUpdate. For blue/green and canary in K8s you use Argo Rollouts or Flagger.

questions.stem: The Bicep equivalent of `terraform plan` is:
questions.explanation: what-if shows the diff without applying. validate only checks syntactic/template validity.

questions.stem: Which deployment mode in ARM/Bicep DELETES resources in the resource group that aren't in the template?
questions.explanation: Complete mode is destructive — use with care. Incremental (default) only adds/updates.

questions.stem: To safely roll out a database schema change without breaking older app versions, use:

options.text: Apply schema before deploying app code

questions.explanation: Expand → migrate data → app uses new schema → contract. Allows mixed-version states during deploy/rollback.

questions.stem: Which Azure Pipelines feature stores reusable variables across pipelines?
questions.explanation: Library variable groups are shareable across pipelines and can be Key Vault-linked for secret hygiene.

questions.stem: Which Azure DevOps integration is the recommended way to consume Azure Key Vault secrets in a pipeline?

options.text: Library variable group linked to a Key Vault
SSH them in from a build agent

questions.explanation: Linked variable groups pull secrets at job-run time. Rotation in KV is invisible to pipelines.

questions.stem: Which scan type analyzes source code for vulnerabilities before runtime?
questions.explanation: SAST = Static Application Security Testing — reads code statically. DAST tests a running app. SCA inspects dependencies.

questions.stem: Which scan type finds vulnerabilities in your open-source dependencies?
questions.explanation: SCA = Software Composition Analysis (Dependabot, Snyk, etc.).

questions.stem: Why is OIDC federation preferred over a Service Principal secret?

options.text: No long-lived secret to rotate or leak

questions.explanation: OIDC issues short-lived tokens via federated trust. Nothing sensitive stored.

questions.stem: Which are valid effects on a pipeline `secret` variable? (Choose two.)

options.text: Not auto-exposed as environment variables to scripts
Stored in plain text in the database

questions.explanation: Secrets are masked + require explicit env: mapping in scripts. They're encrypted at rest.

questions.stem: GHAS for Azure DevOps provides which capabilities? (Choose three.)
questions.explanation: GHAS = secret + code + dependency scanning. Performance profiling is unrelated.

questions.stem: Tools like gitleaks catch ALL secrets in your repo.
questions.explanation: Scanners catch patterns. Random long tokens without a known pattern may slip through. Defense in depth required.

questions.stem: To produce a Software Bill of Materials for a container image, use:
questions.explanation: Syft / Trivy / cdxgen generate SBOMs in CycloneDX or SPDX format.

questions.stem: The three pillars of observability are:
questions.explanation: Metrics (numeric time series), logs (events), traces (request path across services).

questions.stem: Application Insights auto-instruments which languages without code changes? (Choose three.)
questions.explanation: Codeless auto-instrumentation is available for .NET, Java, Node, Python on App Service. Assembly does not.

questions.stem: Which task adds a release annotation to App Insights from a pipeline?
questions.explanation: ApplicationInsightsAnnotation@1 marks the deploy timestamp on App Insights charts.

questions.stem: Workspace-based Application Insights stores its data in a Log Analytics workspace, not a separate datastore.
questions.explanation: Workspace-based AppI writes to a LAW, unifying queries across the two.

questions.stem: To correlate logs across services in a distributed request, the most important thing to include in every log line is:
questions.explanation: Without a shared trace ID, joining logs across services becomes manual archaeology.

questions.stem: The difference between SLA, SLO, and SLI:

options.text: SLI is the measurement, SLO is the internal target, SLA is the external promise
SLA is the measurement, SLO is the average, SLI is the dashboard
SLA is daily, SLO is monthly, SLI is yearly

questions.explanation: SLI → measured indicator. SLO → internal target. SLA → contractual promise (usually looser than SLO).

questions.stem: If your SLO is 99.9%, your monthly error budget is approximately:
questions.explanation: 0.1% of ~30 days ≈ 43.2 minutes per month.

questions.stem: What's the SRE response to consistently meeting 100% uptime when your SLO is 99.9%?

options.text: Take more risks — ship features faster

questions.explanation: Unused error budget = headroom for risk. Ship faster (or introduce chaos engineering) to use the budget productively.

questions.stem: Good characteristics of an SRE alert: (Choose three.)

options.text: Page only what's worth waking someone for

questions.explanation: Alerting on every blip causes alert fatigue and lowers signal-to-noise.

questions.stem: A blameless postmortem assigns responsibility to the engineer who pushed the bad change.
questions.explanation: Blameless means systems are responsible. X pushed the button, but the system let them — fix the system.

questions.stem: The GitHub Actions equivalent of task: AzureCLI@2 is:
questions.explanation: Auth happens once via azure/login@v2; subsequent run: steps call az against the authenticated session.

questions.stem: For OIDC from GitHub Actions to Azure, the workflow must include:
questions.explanation: Without that permission, GitHub Actions cannot mint the OIDC token. Without that token, the federated trust handshake fails.

questions.stem: Reusable workflows in GitHub Actions are invoked with:
questions.explanation: workflow_call reusable workflows are called with uses: owner/repo/.github/workflows/file.yml@ref.

questions.stem: Feature flags are MOST useful for:
questions.explanation: Flags let you deploy code dark, then enable for some/all users without redeploying.

questions.stem: Valid filter types in Azure App Configuration feature management: (Choose two.)
questions.explanation: Built-in filters: Percentage, TimeWindow, Targeting (groups/users).

questions.stem: Old feature flags should be left in place forever in case rollback is needed.
questions.explanation: Stale flags = dead code branches. Delete release/experiment flags after they're 100% rolled out and stable for 2+ weeks.
